title: A UK-HANK model
summary: The Bank of England released a Macro Technical Paper describing a medium-scale Heterogeneous Agent New Keynesian (HANK) model calibrated to the UK economy, incorporating household heterogeneity, housing, international, and fiscal blocks. The model is used to decompose monetary transmission channels and assess their relative importance across different household groups. Key findings include that mortgagors' consumption is most sensitive to house price changes, and that shifts in household balance sheets over the past two decades have dampened the transmission of interest rate changes to prices.
key_points: Developed a medium-scale UK-HANK model featuring rich household heterogeneity alongside detailed housing, international, and fiscal blocks
Model generates realistic income and wealth distributions and marginal propensities to consume consistent with UK evidence
Monetary transmission was decomposed into key channels; mortgagors' consumption is most sensitive to house price changes across tenure groups
A lower share of sterling invoicing in exports mildly weakens monetary transmission
Balanced-budget fiscal reaction functions strengthen monetary transmission
Shifts in household balance sheets over the past two decades have dampened the transmission of interest rate changes to prices
Authors: Daniel Albuquerque, Ed Hill, Sean Lavender, Jamie Lenney and Alberto Polo; published as Macro Technical Paper No. 7
why_it_matters: This research provides central bankers and macro researchers with a more granular framework for understanding how monetary policy decisions transmit through the economy. The finding that household balance sheet shifts have dampened interest rate transmission suggests the traditional monetary transmission mechanism may be weaker than previously assumed, which has implications for setting appropriate policy rates and understanding inflation dynamics.
macro_context: The paper addresses monetary transmission mechanisms—the pathways through which central bank rate decisions affect inflation and output. The finding that monetary transmission has been dampened by household balance sheet changes over two decades is significant for understanding the current effectiveness of monetary policy and may inform the Bank of England's assessment of appropriate policy settings.
